notes.note: Rutilia, mother of the orator C. Aurelius Cotta, was a stock instance of a parent who outlived a child with composure; Cicero is gathering such exempla for his self-addressed Consolatio after Tullia's death. He presses Atticus, the antiquarian, to confirm the family facts.

notes.anchor: whether Clodia outlived her son, the ex-consul Decimus Brutus
notes.note: Another parent-outliving-child case for the Consolatio: whether Clodia survived her son D. Junius Brutus Callaicus (consul 138 BC). This is the old republican Decimus Brutus, not Caesar's assassin of the same name.

The horti are suburban pleasure-gardens across the Tiber that Cicero is hunting for a site on which to build a fanum (shrine) commemorating his dead daughter Tullia. The obsessive garden-search dominates the Astura letters of spring 45 BC.

notes.anchor: pay interest to the seller for no more than a year
notes.note: Roman large purchases were often financed by carrying the unpaid balance at interest (usura) until cash could be raised. Cicero proposes to take a garden on credit for up to a year rather than liquidate his own income-producing estates.
